Column 2026-04-01

South African Kidney Function Gene Research Reveals New Risk Factors for Global Kidney Disease Prevention

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) has emerged as a major global public health challenge. According to the latest data from the Global Burden of Disease Study, approximately 850 million people worldwide are affected by kidney disease, and CKD is the third

LP
Leo Pan - 潘品樺
  1. 首頁
  3. 專欄
  5. Column

Introduction: A Critical Breakthrough in Global Kidney Disease Prevention

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) has emerged as a major global public health challenge. According to the latest data from the Global Burden of Disease Study, approximately 850 million people worldwide are affected by kidney disease, and CKD is the third fastest-growing cause of death globally, projected to become the fifth leading cause of death by 2040. However, kidney disease research has long suffered from significant regional imbalance: the vast majority of genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have focused on European and Asian populations, leaving African populations' genetic risk factors largely overlooked.

In March 2026, the KidneyGenAfrica research consortium, led by Queen Mary University of London, published the largest-ever genomic study of kidney function in African populations in Nature Communications. This study, covering over 110,000 individuals of African descent, not only discovered several previously unknown kidney function-related genetic loci but also revealed the unique genetic architecture of African populations, opening a new chapter in global kidney disease prevention.

Research Content

Research Team and Methods: A Model of Cross-Continental Collaboration

KidneyGenAfrica is a multinational collaborative research project that pooled genomic data from 10 study cohorts across three geographic regions: East Africa, West Africa, and Southern Africa. The research team included major research institutions such as AWI-Gen (Africa Wits-INDEPTH Partnership for Genomic Studies), UGR (Uganda Genome Resource), AADM (African America Diabetes Study), MEIRU (Malawi Epidemiology and Intervention Research Unit), and ARK (African Research on Kidney Disease).

The study employed a rigorous three-stage meta-analysis approach: the first stage conducted regional meta-analyses separately for East Africa, West Africa, and Southern Africa; the second stage integrated results from all three regions into a continental African meta-analysis; the third stage further incorporated African ancestry data from MVP (Million Veteran Program), UKB (UK Biobank), and the CKDGen consortium, forming a pan-African meta-analysis.

The study used estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR) as the primary indicator of kidney function, calculated using the CKD-EPI formula, excluding race-based correction coefficients to more accurately reflect kidney function status across different populations. The research team applied rigorous quality control to genotype data, including excluding highly related individuals, samples with high genotype missing rates, and variants deviating from Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium.

Risk Factors Discovered: Identification of Novel Genetic Loci

The core finding of this study was the identification of multiple novel genetic loci associated with kidney function, with some loci being monomorphic in European and Asian populations, highlighting the unique value of conducting genetic research in African populations.

Novel Discoveries from Regional Meta-Analysis

In the regional meta-analysis, the research team identified four independent genetic loci reaching genome-wide significance (p < 5 × 10⁻⁸). Most notably, two novel genetic loci were discovered:

OPRM1 Gene Locus (rs73788952): Located near the μ-opioid receptor gene, this is the first discovery of this gene's association with kidney function. The study showed that this variant exhibits significant association in East African populations, with consistent effect directions across different study cohorts. LOC645752 Gene Locus (rs4243063): Located in a gene region with not yet fully understood function, achieving genome-wide significance in Southern African populations.

Additionally, the research team discovered associations with known kidney disease-related genes in East African populations, including HBB (sickle cell-related) and SLC25A45 gene loci.

Breakthrough Discoveries from Pan-African Meta-Analysis

In the pan-African meta-analysis incorporating additional data, the research team identified a total of 19 independent kidney function-related genetic loci. Three loci were reported for the first time in association with kidney function:

ARG1 Gene Locus (rs141647693): Arginase 1 gene, involved in the urea cycle, closely related to kidney metabolic function. SORD2P Gene Locus (rs12595073): Located upstream of the GATM gene, potentially affecting kidney function by regulating neighboring genes. SQRDL Gene Locus (rs1918516): Sulfide quinone oxidoreductase-like gene, involved in cellular redox balance.

Notably, the GATM gene (guanidinoacetate N-methyltransferase) was one of the most replicated findings in the study. This gene participates in creatine biosynthesis and has previously been found associated with kidney function, creatinine levels, and chronic kidney disease in European and Asian population GWAS. This study confirmed this association in African populations for the first time.

APOL1 Gene: A Double-Edged Sword of Protection and Risk

The study also conducted an in-depth exploration of the well-known APOL1 gene variants' distribution in African populations and their association with kidney function. The APOL1 gene encodes apolipoprotein L1, with its G1 and G2 high-risk alleles being more frequent in African-ancestry populations.

Key findings include:

  • G1 allele frequency: East Africa 6.8%, Southern Africa 9.9%, West Africa 12.2%
  • G2 allele frequency: East Africa 7.7%, West Africa 10%, Southern Africa 16.6%
  • High-risk genotype frequency: Highest in Southern Africa (7.4%), followed by West Africa (4.2%), lowest in East Africa (2.2%)

These alleles originated from protective effects against African sleeping sickness (caused by Trypanosoma brucei), possessing natural selection advantages. However, they simultaneously increase the risk of chronic kidney disease, HIV-associated nephropathy, and focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

In association analysis with low eGFR, the research team found that APOL1 high-risk genotypes were significantly associated with kidney function decline in East African populations (dominant model OR = 1.54, 95% CI = 1.09–2.18; recessive model OR = 4.38, 95% CI = 2.12–9.03). However, Southern and West African populations had limited statistical power due to fewer low eGFR cases.

Challenges and Breakthroughs in Polygenic Risk Scores

The research team evaluated the predictive performance of polygenic scores (PGSs) from different sources in the Malawi MEIRU population. Results showed that PGS derived from Southern African populations performed best in predicting local population kidney function (R² = 0.11%, p = 0.037), outperforming PGS from East Africa, West Africa, pan-African, and even multi-ancestry sources.

This finding challenges the traditional notion that "larger sample sizes produce better PGS," emphasizing the critical role of genetic distance in PGS development and application. The study demonstrates that when training and validation populations are genetically closer, PGS predictive performance significantly improves.

Implications for Global Kidney Disease Prevention

This study has multiple implications for global kidney disease prevention:

Filling Research Gaps: For a long time, genomic research has been heavily biased toward European and Asian populations, with African populations' genetic diversity under-studied. This study increased African population GWAS sample size eightfold, providing critical data for understanding human kidney function genetic architecture. Discovering Population-Specific Variants: Multiple genetic loci (such as rs6670659, rs73788952, rs1706775) are common in African populations but monomorphic in European and Asian populations. This means studies relying solely on Eurasian populations cannot discover these important genetic factors. Revealing Genetic Heterogeneity: The study found significant differences in genetic architecture across different African geographic regions, explaining why certain genetic loci cannot be replicated in other regions. This regional specificity has important implications for precision medicine practice. Improving Risk Prediction Tools: The research provides a foundation for developing kidney disease risk prediction tools suitable for African populations, helping to identify high-risk individuals early and implement preventive interventions.

South African Perspective: Why This Research Comes from South Africa

A Treasure Trove of Genetic Diversity

South Africa possesses the world's highest genetic diversity. Humans originated in Africa, and over tens of thousands of years of evolution, African populations have accumulated rich genetic variation. This diversity makes South Africa an ideal location for conducting genetic research.

Studies show that genetic differences within African populations are even greater than differences between Africa and Eurasia. This means studying African populations can reveal more genetic variants that are absent or rare in other populations.

The Reality of Disease Burden

Chronic kidney disease imposes a heavy burden in South Africa. According to Global Burden of Disease Study data, kidney disease-related mortality rates remain high in South Africa, and patients often present symptoms at younger ages—5 to 20 years earlier on average compared to European and Asian patients.

This high disease burden has complex underlying causes: the dual burden of infectious diseases (such as HIV, hepatitis B) and non-communicable diseases (such as hypertension, diabetes), food and water insecurity, occupational environmental exposures, heat stress, and climate change all combine to create unique kidney disease risks for South African populations.

Development of Research Infrastructure

In recent years, South Africa has invested substantial resources in genomic research. The AWI-Gen study (Africa Wits-INDEPTH Partnership for Genomic Studies) established research sites in South Africa, Ghana, Kenya, and Burkina Faso, laying the foundation for large-scale genomic research.

South African scientists have extensive experience in kidney disease research. Programs like the African Research on Kidney Disease (ARK) have long tracked local population health status, providing valuable phenotype data.

Talent Development and Capacity Building

The KidneyGenAfrica project is not just scientific research but also a capacity-building platform. While conducting genomic research, the research team trained local scientists, established biobanks and data analysis infrastructure, and laid the foundation for future research.

Global Impact: Implications for Other Regions

Significance for the African Diaspora

Hundreds of millions of African diaspora populations exist globally, primarily distributed in the Americas, Europe, and the Caribbean. Due to the historical slave trade, these populations carry genetic variations similar to African continental populations, including APOL1 high-risk alleles.

Studies show that African Americans have three times the risk of kidney failure compared to other populations, partly due to APOL1 high-risk genotypes. The genetic data from this study can help these populations better understand their genetic risks and develop more precise screening and prevention strategies.

Implications for Precision Medicine

This study has profound implications for precision medicine development:

Population Specificity is Critical: Different populations may have different genetic risk factors, and one-size-fits-all health strategies may exacerbate health inequalities. The study calls for considering population diversity when developing diagnostic tools and treatment protocols. PGS Needs Localization: Polygenic score predictive performance highly depends on genetic similarity between training data and target populations. The study recommends developing population-specific PGS rather than simply applying European population tools to other groups. Early Screening Strategies: Understanding population-specific genetic risk factors enables more targeted screening strategies, implementing more intensive monitoring in high-risk populations.

Implications for Public Health Policy

Research findings have direct implications for public health policy:

Resource Allocation: In regions with high APOL1 high-risk allele frequencies, kidney disease screening and treatment resources should be increased. Prevention Strategies: Understanding genetic risks allows for more effective prevention strategies targeting modifiable environmental factors (such as blood pressure control, water quality improvement, occupational exposure reduction). Health Education: Providing genetic risk counseling to high-risk populations helps individuals make informed lifestyle choices.

Implications for Drug Development

The genetic loci discovered in this study may become targets for new drug development. For example, the GATM gene's involvement in creatine metabolism may provide new approaches for kidney disease treatment. Understanding different populations' genetic architecture also helps design more inclusive clinical trials, ensuring drug safety and efficacy across different populations.

Conclusion: Future Prospects

The KidneyGenAfrica study marks an important milestone in kidney disease genetic research, but this is just the beginning. Future research directions include:

Longitudinal Studies and Phenotype Deepening

This study is primarily based on cross-sectional design, relying on single eGFR measurements. Future longitudinal follow-up studies are needed to more accurately define chronic kidney disease (requiring two or more eGFR measurements more than three months apart) and explore the impact of genetic factors on kidney function decline rates.

Functional Validation Studies

Identified genetic loci require functional validation studies to clarify their roles in kidney disease pathogenesis. This includes gene editing models, cellular experiments, and molecular mechanism studies.

Population Expansion Studies

Although this study covered East Africa, West Africa, and Southern Africa, the African continent's genetic diversity is far from fully captured. Future research should expand to North Africa, Central Africa, and other regions, incorporating more population-specific cohorts.

Clinical Translation Research

Translating research findings into clinical applications requires multiple efforts: developing validated PGS tools, formulating population-specific clinical guidelines, establishing genetic counseling services, and training healthcare professionals to understand and apply genetic information.

Global Collaboration Platforms

Kidney disease prevention requires global cooperation. The collaborative framework established by KidneyGenAfrica can serve as a model, promoting cross-national, cross-continental research collaboration in other disease areas. Data sharing, standardized phenotype collection, and fair benefit distribution principles will be key to the success of these collaborations.

Summary

The South Africa-led KidneyGenAfrica study represents an important step in the transition of genetic research from "Europe-centered" to "globally inclusive." By conducting the largest genomic study of kidney function on the African continent, scientists discovered multiple previously unknown genetic risk factors, revealed the unique genetic architecture of African populations, and provided key insights for precision medicine application in diverse populations.

Against the backdrop of continuously rising global kidney disease burden, this study not only helps improve kidney disease prevention in African populations but also provides an important scientific foundation for global public health. In the future, with deeper research and clinical translation advancement, we have reason to expect more precise and equitable kidney disease prevention strategies, bringing new hope to 850 million kidney disease patients worldwide.

---

Author

Leo Pan

CEO - DingYao Advisory

Leo Pan has extensive background in health technology and genomics research, with long-standing attention to global public health issues and precision medicine development. He is dedicated to translating cutting-edge scientific research into publicly understandable knowledge, promoting health information accessibility. As CEO of DingYao Advisory, he leads the team in driving health innovation solutions, with particular focus on genetic disease risk assessment and prevention strategy localization. In writing this article about South African kidney function gene research, he combined professional understanding of genomics with deep concern for global health inequalities, hoping to help more readers understand the important implications of this breakthrough research for global kidney disease prevention.

---

References

1. KidneyGenAfrica Consortium. (2026). KidneyGenAfrica multi-cohort Genome-wide association study and polygenic prediction of kidney function in 110,000 Africans. Nature Communications.

2. Global Burden of Disease Study 2023. Global, regional, and national burden of chronic kidney disease in adults, 1990-2023.

3. Gbadegesin et al. (2024). APOL1 Bi- and Monoallelic Variants and Chronic Kidney Disease in West Africans. New England Journal of Medicine.

4. George C, et al. (2018). An African perspective on the genetic risk of chronic kidney disease: a systematic review. BMC Medical Genetics.

5. CKDGen Consortium. Multi-ancestry genome-wide association study of kidney function.
